video.title: How Did Chris Froome Win The Vuelta A España? | The GCN Show Ep. 244

all_candidates.title: How Did Chris Froome Win The Vuelta A España? | The GCN Show Ep. 244

transcript.clean_text: - Live from the Grand Prix Cycliste de Montreal. - Welcome to the GCN show! - Whoo hoo! - Welcome to the GCN show, from Alta Badia in the Dolomites. - This week, we delve deep and take a look at how Chris Froome really won the Vuelta. - At that very same race, Alberto Contador retires in some style, whilst Peter Sagan in Canada takes his 100th career victory. - Not so stylish, however, is the bicycle umbrella, which we'll take a look at later. All the while, Mark Beaumont is getting ever closer to the end of his around-the-world adventure. - We learned that Chris Froome is a modern-day cannibal, more on that later on. We also learned that Alberto Contador is true to his word and indeed to his stem because he did indeed attack all the way to the end, and it paid off in dividends, didn't it? He won on the Angliru, no less. - Thankfully, Dan, that quote or phrase was on his stem cap in English, unlike-- - It was. - The slightly Spanish issue that we had with your querer es poder-- - Oh yeah. - Translation. A few people picked up on that. - Uh, I'm not particularly good at Spanish, as you can probably tell from my pronunciation just then, but that means "love is power." Love is power. Love is power. Yes, well, I'm gonna blame Google Translate, as well as, of course, my own Spanish. - Do you mean your non-existent-- - That is the one-- - Spanish? - Yes, it is non-existent. - I think the thing that I really love about Alberto Contador is, when you look back at his Grand Tour results, whenever he finished on the podium of a Grand Tour, he won it. - Yeah. - He was never second and never third. I think we're really gonna miss his do-or-die attitude and the way he shook up every race. - Yeah, he was certainly fun to watch. I think some of us are going to miss him more than others though. In particular, I'm talking about Matt. It's the end of a bromance. - Come on, let's go! - Cheerio. - Good bye. - All the best, cheers. - See you. - sake. - Also this week, Chris Froome has done the double in winning the Vuelta last week as well as the Tour de France beforehand. He's become the ninth rider in history to win two Grand Tours in a single season, and in fact, the first rider to do so since Marco Pantani 19 years ago. - That makes me feel very old. - Yeah, imagine how it makes Matt Stephens feel. - It's a super impressive feat, I think there can be no doubt about that, but what I really want to know is how did he finally do it, because he has been trying to win this for years. - Well, it's an interesting one, isn't it? And I think we should firstly look at what he did before the Vuelta, and actually before the Tour de France itself, because a lot has been made about how he was physically and mentally fresher going into the Tour this year, but when you delve into the stats themselves, I'm not really sure that that's the exact reason why he did so well. - Yeah, I think you're right, because when you look at it, he did have his fewest number of racing days before a Tour de France win this year. So, he raced for 26 days before the tour started, but last year and in 2015, he had 27 racing days, So again, not too big a difference. 2014, he had 28, in 2013, he had 31, but then, in 2012, when he was second behind then teammate Bradley Wiggins, he only had 20 days of racing. So, I don't really think we can say that it is the days of racing. - No, I think you're right there, and I personally would put this year's success more down to sheer determination, some very careful planning, but also, some very clear goal-setting, because as far as I remember, this has been the first year where Froome, and Team Sky, have openly declared that they want to do the double i.e. They want to win the Tour, and they want to go on and win the Vuelta. - I see exactly what you're saying there, because the Vuelta, not just for Froome, but for many other Grand Tour favourites, often feels like a bit of an afterthought. The attitude is probably, "Well, let's see how we "feel after the Tour de France and go from there," but this year, the team were very clear in their goal, and they executed it. - Yeah, and the team actually a very important part part of Froome's success as well this year, weren't they? - What we're gonna have to do though is just give the kudos to Chris Froome that he deserves, because he's getting more and more astute as his racing years go on. And also, he's just oh so consistent. Like, whenever he has a bad day, it's not really that much of a bad day, is it? Especially compared to bad days that his rivals have, and what that means is that he appears unbeatable in Grand Tours. - Yeah, and when he gets the chance to take seconds, he always does it, doesn't he? And he also takes the chance to grab points whenever he can as well, as we learnt on Sunday. Such is his appetite for victories and wins at the moment that he sprinted for points on the very last day, denying Matteo Trentin the green jersey and taking it for himself. This, and these results that are coming with that determination, are putting Froome right up there with the best riders in history. - I actually think he should be applauded for his cannibal-like attitude as well. - Yeah, yeah, we don't criticise Merckx, do we? - No. - From that point of view. - It's now time for Cycling Shorts. - We shall start Cycling Shorts this week with some brand-new cycling shorts, and that is because Team Movistar have already decided to release their 2018 team kit. I'm not sure what you lot think about that, but I am personally giving that a thumbs up, I like it. - Yeah, let us know your thoughts, though. Hot or not in the poll, which is right above Dan's head, about now. More fantastic news from Movistar 2018 though, is the fact that, that next year, they will have a women's team. - Yeah. - So, they joined the likes of fellow World Tour teams Lotto Soudal, Sunweb, Orica, and FDJ in having a men's team and a women's team running on concurrent programmes. I think, you know, hopefully, we'll see more teams follow in their steps. - Well, let's hope so. Maybe, even all teams by 2020, what do you reckon? - I think parity by 2020-- - Yeah, keep my fingers-- - Sounds good, doesn't it? - Crossed. Right onto a study that I was looking at the other day from the Swiss Side aerodynamics team. I take a look at this graph, which puts into picture format the results they got whilst they were in a wind tunnel recently. - The remarkable thing about this, Dan, is just how similar it is to the results that you and Matt got from your GCN Does Science drafting behind-- - Can't believe we-- - motos video. - Ever doubted it! - That massive coincidence aside, the two kind of key takeaways from this, for me, were just how much of a difference drafting can make. So, even if you're 10 metres behind the wheel, which is actually incidentally, the legal distance in non-drafting triathlons, at 45 kilometres an hour and still conditions, you can save 13 percent of your power. - Yeah, crazy. - Which is huge! And then, if you go 20 metres off the wheel in the same conditions, you're still saving nine percent. - Yeah, that was the one that got me, because 20 metres measured out is an enormous distance. You would never think that you'd get any benefit-- - No, not at all. - Riding that far behind somebody. Also though, even without anybody in front of you, if you've just got a rider sat on your wheel, you are saving over four percent in terms of your power output at 45 kilometres per hour, so next time, you've got a wheel-sucker sat behind you, don't bemoan them, because they are actually helping you to go faster. - I'm not sure they're helping you quite as much as they would be if they took the occasional turn on the front. - Well, hey, that is very true, but still. - I'm not going above a certain power, I've got to stay in my zones. - The power that just happens to correspond with sitting on the wheels? - Yeah, exactly, perfect. - Coincidence. - Moving from drafting to an event where drafting absolutely isn't allowed, Sarah Hammond is currently leading the Race to the Rock event over in Australia. She's covered two and a half thousand kilometres over rugged terrain in just over nine days at the time of filming this and has a comfortable lead over Kevin Benkenstein of South Africa. - Sticking with epic rides, kind of, Matt and Si have got some news for you. So, we'll hand over to them right now. - Really sorry that me and Si couldn't be in this week's GCN show. - Yeah. - We're actually out panic-training because, in around a month's time, we're taking part in the KOM Challenge in Taiwan, which is basically a 105-kilometer hill climb. - Yeah, we start out at sea level, and we wind our way up to the summit of Taiwan's highest road pass, which is 3275 metres. - But, so it's not all that bad, 'cause the first 20K-- - Yeah. - Well, the average gradient there is only two percent. - Okay. - And then the next 75 kilometres, well, that's only a mere six percent. - 75 kilometres is at six percent? - Yeah. - Yeah, what are we worried about, mate? - Well, actually, come to think of it, the last 9.5 kilometres, well, it averages between 10 and 22 percent. - Okay, all right, I'm worried about that. I'm also worried about-- - So am I. - The fact that we're lining up against Vincenzo Nibali, Cadel Evans, and Emma Pooley. - We won't need to worry about those guys for too long, mate, we're going all right. - We are going all right. We also, we got two secret weapons, Matt. - Have we? - I couldn't help but notice, there's no weight restriction on the KOM Challenge. So, we've got two very special Factor bikes with lightweight wheels and THM components. - Boom! - Pew! - I can't see us being beat! - No way. Stick that-- - Bring on Nibs! - In your pipe and smoke it, Nibali. - He's got no chance, does he? No chance. - I tell you what, Lasty, I hope, for their sakes, that there is no time limit at the Taiwan KOM Challenge. - And for our sakes as well, we wouldn't want them to let our good name down, really-- - No. - Would we? What I also hope, Dan, is that there aren't any farmers there. - You what? - Did you not read the article in the Irish Independent? - No. - Two farmers dressed in flat caps and tweed jackets-- - You mean two farmers dressed up as farmers, Lasty? - Yeah. Two farmers dressed up as farmers attempt to disrupt a race in Scotland. They stood in the middle of the road, brandishing sticks, trying to block the riders going through. - Oh good, and you get all sorts out there these days, don't you? Well, let's hope that our Mark Beaumont doesn't come across any such farmers either. He now well and truly has the end in sight, the end being Paris, almost eighty days now after he started. So, let's check in with him to find out the latest. - Hello, GCN, quick catch-up from the road on the Artemis World Cycle, this is Day 65, and I've made it to Wisconsin. So, yeah, pretty significant, because, well, I've dropped from Canada into the US now. I've crossed the prairies. I was hoping the prairies would be my fastest miles. I had three or four days absolutely battering into a headwind. There was a day there where I did shy of 200 miles in 16 hours. I mean, you can do the sum if it was brutal. And by then, you know, it switched around, and there was two days where I pushed 270 miles each day, so the margin of error is getting less and less. Obviously, I'm gonna come in pretty close to the 80 days. Keep in mind, the current world record is 123 days, I'm trying to get around the planet 18000 miles in less than 80. So, yeah, if you're, if you're wanting to follow the day-to-day and see how it's going, then, obviously I'm on Strava, strava.com/MarkBeaumont. And the main site, so you can actually see the tracking map, and you can see progress and all the other media is artemisworldcycle.com. So, yeah, thanks for the support, GCN. - You know, Dan, I still can't get my head around what Mark's doing. - No. - It's absolutely incredible. - We've said it before, and we will no doubt say it again, but what he is doing is, quite frankly, mind-boggling. You wouldn't catch me going anywhere near something like that. One record that was already broken last week was the JOGLEJOG Record, Lasty. I'm sure we saw that. - The, the what? - John o' Groats to Land's End and back, what a popular record in the UK, although, traditionally, done at the other way around. The record was broken by a certain James MacDonald, and we understand that, by the Guinness World Record rules, it is allowed to do it in reverse. He covered the 1850 miles in five days, 18 hours, and three minutes, taking a clear three hours off the previous record, which has stood for seven years. A well done to you, James. - Yeah, well done, James! And a bit of racing news now, and we're gonna have to start off with some bad news, which is Orica-Scott's incredibly popular Backstage Pass series is unfortunately coming to an end. The man behind it, Dan Jones, is taking a step away from the team in order to spend more time for his family. I think sort of a bit of a loss for cycling fans, well, all cycling fans, really. I always found the videos to be emotional and informative, but more than anything, just really good fun to watch. - Well, yeah, most definitely, and I don't doubt that the fan base of Orica-Scott wouldn't be anywhere near what it is without those Backstage Pass videos. So, thank you very much to Dan Jones. He did a great job there. Two more people who are leaving our sport, at least as professional riders, are Andrew Talansky, who announced his surprise retirement at the age of just 28 from the Cannondale-Drapac team, and his former teammate, Tyler Farrar, who is a little bit older, but he is now ready for life outside of cycling. So, good luck to both of you moving forward as well. - There was, though, some absolutely fantastic news for Talansky's now former team, Cannondale-Drapac. Swedish firm Education First, who're all about teaching people how to communicate, have come onboard as the main naming sponsor for the team from January 1st, 2018, and with that, we understand that the team have been able to retain the services of their three stars, Rigoberto Uran, Sep Vanmarcke, and Michael Woods. - Yeah, that was great news and a big relief wasn't it? Long live the Argyle Army. Cheers. - Cheers, Dan. - Last week, we had a tech overload for you. This week, however, it's not Hack or Bodge. It may well look like that, but we have got some treats for you, mainly from Kickstarter. First up is the FlexiSpot Deskcise Pro. Now, this is actually getting very close to its funding target of $50000. It's essentially an exercise bike with a desk built in, so you can work out while you work. Sounds pretty cool to me. Might even ask the boss if I can have one. Next up, it's the bike umbrella. Now, according to them, cycling always gets complicated on rainy days. Essentially, this product does exactly what a rain jacket does, although there is a mount for your bike umbrella as well, so you can take it wherever you go. I could have done with that on the ride to work this morning, when it absolutely chucked it down with rain. Moving on from Kickstarter to this, the Sada folding bike. It also goes down so small, it can fit inside of a rucksack. Granted, there is a bit of work to do on this as well, and the designers have even said that, but it certainly looks, well, interesting, doesn't it? Would you have one? Let us know in the comments down below. Back to Dan and Tom in the lovely Alta Badia. I could just dream of that. - Believe it or not, we had an amazing view when we sat down to record the GCN Show. Taking us long time, Last, isn't it? Right, let's crack on with racing news, and on Friday, Peter Sagan took the 100th victory of his career over at the Grand Prix Quebec in Canada. In the repeat results of last year, he got the better of Greg Van Avermaet in an uphill sprint to the line. - Yeah, so with his 100th win, it's interesting to use ProCyclingStats' as feature, where you can compare riders' victories at certain ages to other riders. So, Mark Cavendish, at the same age as Sagan, had 93 victories. Tom Boonen had 65, and Marcel Kittel had 60. So, I'm not sure whether we needed concrete proof that Sagan is a class bike rider, but there it is. - Yeah, well, it puts it into context, doesn't it? A couple of days later, Diego Ulissi, the Italian rider, took the first ever one-day World Tour race victory of his career by winning the Grand Prix Montreal. He was part of a six-man group, which went clear in the closing stages, and in the world time sprints, he got the better of Jesus Herrada and an in-form Tom-Jelte Slagter. - Thomas De Gendt finally took a Grand Tour stage win at the Vuelta Espana-- - Yeah, you're right. - As well. On stage 19, he took a win from the break, and afterwards, he tweeted this. - Oh yeah, hold on. - "I did 40 stages in the grand tours "this year, 15 stages I was in the break. "I win the last attempt. "I wish someone could have told me that." - Yeah, yeah, we did like that, didn't we? It didn't come without its cost though. Later, he also tweeted a picture, which we'll show you shortly, there is no doubt that when he takes a well-earned holiday at the end of this season, he's going to stand out from the crowd. Take a look at this. - That man needs a long pair of swimming shorts. - Yeah. - Matteo Trentin rounded out a fine Vuelta Espana for himself and for his Quick-Step team with a win on the final day in Madrid. That took his total to four stage wins at the race and the team's total to six stage wins. However, Trentin's four wins weren't quite enough to deliver him the overall in the points jersey. - No, no, they weren't. The previous day was all about one man, that being, of course, Alberto Contador. The script is kind of written for that stage before it even started, and unlike his bestie Matt Stephens, he was able to stick to the script. He took a very famous and very popular win up the infamous Angliru climb, and as such, as you can probably guess, he is this week's Wattage Bazooka! - Meanwhile, this week's viewer Wattage Bazooka was nominated by Phil Tromans, and it goes to 14-year-old Tom Pughe-Morgan, who recently Everested by climbing a hill called Ditchling Beacon that's in the South of England 65 times. - Oh my goodness, wow, well done to you, Tom. That's all I can say. If you would like to nominate anyone for next week's show, you just need to use the #wattagebazooka on social media, and we shall pick our favourite next week. Onto the Tour of Britain, now, and that is won for a second time by Lars Boom, the Dutchman. He had dominated the individual 16-kilometer time trial to take the race lead, and he and his team valiantly defended that all the way through to the finish. - At the Lotto Belgium Tour, Anouska Koster took the overall win, following a very powerful and very brave attack on the final stages. So, she not only took the stage win, she also got the overall. At the Madrid Challenge, Belgian champion Jolien D'Hoore out-sprinted Coryn Rivera for the sprint. At that very same race, Anna van der Breggen also wrapped up the overall in the Women's WorldTour despite not actually taking part in the race, such was her dominance earlier in the season. - Mm-hmm, and before we finish with racing news this week, we are going to have to give a shout out to one, Annie Last, sister of the Brick here, and we normally leave these results to our friends over at the Global Mountain Bike Network, but Annie picked up a fine silver medal at the Elite Women's Cross Country World Championships over in Cairns at the weekend. Jolanda Neff took the win there, and it was Pauline Ferrand Prevot who finished behind Annie in third place, quite remarkable indeed. I think, from this point onwards, Lasty, I'm afraid you will be known as the Brother of Annie. - That's good, it's better than the outtake where you called me the Sister of Annie. - Yeah, that is true. - The view is back, and it's time for Hack forward-slash Bodge of the Week, and first up, this is a classic. It was spotted by Daniel Hawthorne, so it's not Daniel's bike, but he sent it in to us on Facebook. - Wow. - Where to start, Dan? - Well, I mean, that does look very aero on the chainstays, seatstays, and top tube. He hasn't put so much attention to detail on the down tube. - But I don't know what the fairing's made out of-- - No. - Is that plastic or something? - What you liked, Lasty, is the fact that the chain goes through the fairing at the back. - I just think the attention to detail there is next-level, it's a hack for me. - Well, it's a hack for me as well. That thing does look remarkable. Next up, Jonathan Hall, he tried to use his dad's old mudguards for his uni commuter bike, but the front one didn't fit because of the disc brake callipers. So, he's put an old ass saver on there, and I think that looks particularly neat, I have to say, very clean bike thus far. Don't think he's done much commuting yet. - So, hack or bodge? - Hack. - I was gonna give it a bodge for this bit. - Well, you decide. - There we go, Niels Peetermans has made a bike-shaped pinata. - Aw, that's cool, yeah, that's gotta be a hack, isn't it? - Definite, definite hack, Niels. Definite hack. - Right, next up, we have this one from Mark Van Suchtelen. He has sent in this, where he has decluttered his front-end by using some velcro to mount his Campagnolo EPS junction box. That is definitely a hack from me. - That is cool, that is cool, but I just want to draw your attention to the number of spacers underneath the stem, Dan. - Oh dear, yeah, maybe he should have cropped that. - Okay, anyway, next up from the Dandy Narwahls, who arrived on a bike trip to Albania with a broken seat post and bodged it by wedging what looks like two bits of Allen key-- - Oh yeah? - Underneath the rails. - So, they cut it down, too. So presumably, that works. - Apparently, it held. It's a bodge, isn't it? - It's a bodge, but that's what you need-- - Stone-cold bodge, but it's good. - In that sort of situation there, just a bodge to get you through. - True. - Justin Hemming, well, this is a real bodge, surely. Seen on Facebook, #GCNhack, looks like a tow bar from a car used as a pedal. - Is it? - You could probably get away with it, couldn't you, but it's definitely a bodge. - 100% bodge. Finally, Reilly Pigeon sent this one in, who said-- - Oh wow. - "Got a few of these Sephora mascara brushes while shopping with my girlfriend. - Good brand, that Sephora. - Good brand. Turns out they're actually quite useful for cleaning the bits in between your chain. - Yeah, well, if you want to go the extra mile in chain-cleaning, that looks like the brush for you, doesn't it? - It certainly does, that's next-level attention to detail, that's a hack for me. - Right, if you've got any hacks or bodges that you would like to send us ready for next week's show, all you've got to do is use the hashtag GCNhack on Twitter or Instagram. Competition time now, and as you'll be well aware, we've had some amazing competitions for you recently. So numerous are the winners that we are going to have them rolling across the screen as opposed to reading them out. So, the names that you see now are the five lucky winners of the Fi'zi:k competition, each of whom have won their choice of Fi'zi:k shoes and saddle. - We also had a Zwift competition with the Zwift Academy where we had 10 indoor trainers up for grabs. Registration for the Zwift Academy is open to all and is open until September the 15th, and there could also be two pro team places up for grabs as part of that. - Yeah, that's an opportunity of a lifetime, is it? Make sure that you do do that. The five names now scrolling across the screen are the winners of the Tacx Neo, and once they've finished about now, the next five names are the winners of the Wahoo KICKR SNAP, well done to all of you. - Finally, for winners this week, the winner of the Kask Protone helmet is Innes Browning. All of our competitions haven't quite closed yet, so if you'd like to win a new Wattbike Atom, there is a link to enter that competition down in the description. - Yeah, and the winner of the Lightweight Wheels competition is going to be announced next week, sorry. - Caption of the Week now, and last week's photo was this. - Yeah, and we have a winner, and that winner is Ed Markey with caption, "Siri, where did I leave my helmet?" Well done to you Ed, get in touch with us on Facebook with your address, and we'll get a GCN CamelBak water bottle out to you ASAP. - This week's photo is this one of Peter Sagan, and if you don't mind, Dan, I'm gonna start. - I've got some, too, but yeah, you get to start-- - Okay. - It. - Okay, thanks, thanks. So, my caption is, "Peter Sagan, clearly pumped for his 100th pro victory." - Very good, very good, I've got a couple of songs. First up-- - Great. ♫ Don't you know, pump it up ♫ You've got to pump it up ♫ Don't you, et cetera And also ♫ Pump up the jam, pump it up ♫ Whilst your feet are pumping ♫ Over there, the jam is pumping Et cetera, et cetera as well, if you can do better than that, which I very much doubt you can, you can leave your captions in the comments section just down below. - I just, I just can't imagine you being beaten. Please do comment with your efforts though. - And please stay for the rest of the show, Lasty. - Please stay. - Don't be embarrassed. Coming up on the channel this week, on Wednesday, we are going to show you how to utilise your summer cycling form for some events in the fall. - Now, when you do start racing, a cross event lasts between about 40 minutes to an hour, so if you're coming off a good summer of riding and racing, then actually, the duration will be no problem to you. So, instead, to convert that summer form to cross form, you need to concentrate on two things: short, sharp, intense efforts and your technique. - Thursday, we've got our top five cycling tantrums. - No! - And on Friday, as ever, it is AskGCANything. - On Saturday, we take a look at Rohan Dennis' time trial bike as he builds up for the time trial Worlds. On Sunday, we have got a very cool factory tour, and on Monday, there's a maintenance video where Si takes a look at the impact of stem set-up. - Yeah, now on Tuesday, there's once again the GCN Show and, once again, from Alta Badia. - Look at the view. - Getting better, isn't it? - From the top of the A27 overbridge in Flevoland, Holland, at 2200 millimetres above sea level, welcome to the GCN Show. - Right, well, that brings this week's GCN Show to a close, we hope you've enjoyed it. If you have, please give it the thumbs up just down below. Great to have Lasty back, I think you will agree. If you haven't subscribed to the channel, please now click on the globe, and that is exactly what you will do for free. - And if you'd like to check out a couple of videos, I thoroughly recommend Dan's Top Five Cycling Rivalries, Part Two, which is right there, and also, as it's the start of the cyclo-cross season, why not check out the masterclass from the master, Sven Nys, right there. There's also a link to our shop on screen, so click there, too.
transcript.preview_start: - Live from the Grand Prix Cycliste de Montreal. - Welcome to the GCN show! - Whoo hoo! - Welcome to the GCN show, from Alta Badia in the Dolomites. - This week, we delve deep and take a look at how Chris Froome really won the Vuelta. - At that very same race, Alberto Contador retires in some style, whilst Peter Sagan in Canada takes his 100th career victory. - Not so stylish, however, is the bicycle umbrella, which we'll take a look at later. All the while, Mark Beaumont is getting ever closer
